code.risk: critical: Delayed IM epinephrine, reliance on antihistamines, or an erroneous IV bolus can allow rapid airway collapse, refractory shock, or a fatal dosing error.
high: Waiting for neurologic recovery in a shocked post-VF patient with persistent ST elevation can delay definitive treatment of an occluded coronary artery.
critical: Giving epinephrine before initial shocks, redosing too rapidly, or substituting vasopressin can distract from the interventions most strongly linked to survival in shockable arrest.
critical: Using a perfusing-VT infusion regimen during pulseless VF, pausing CPR, or delaying defibrillation for drug administration can materially reduce resuscitation quality.
critical: Delaying calcium in ECG-toxic hyperkalemia risks degeneration to VF or asystole; failing to monitor glucose or arrange dialysis leaves major treatment-related and rebound hazards.
critical: Treating beta-blocker exposure as a reason to delay IM epinephrine can permit rapid progression to airway obstruction, cardiovascular collapse, and cardiac arrest.
high: Persistent hyperoxemia, untreated hypotension, uncontrolled fever, or missed seizures can compound neurologic and organ injury during the vulnerable post-arrest period.

code_stroke_validation.risk: Blood-pressure agents, concentrations, and escalation paths vary by institution; the AHA/ASA thresholds are retained without prescribing a site-specific regimen.
AHA guidance defines resuscitation priorities and medication timing, but record ownership and correction workflows are local operational policies.
Exact crash-cart inventory, sealing, beyond-use, and release requirements are institution-specific; the case tests observable readiness hazards without declaring a universal cart layout.
The clinical dose is guideline-based, but competency scoring, event reporting, and the number or format of repeat attempts are local program decisions.
Reperfusion selection can incorporate advanced imaging and other patient factors; this case validates timeline accuracy rather than declaring treatment eligibility from time alone.
Tenecteplase is a bolus and cannot be stopped after administration; hemorrhage workup and reversal details depend on patient findings, product availability, and local protocol.
AHA guidance supports high-quality CPR, short pauses, and team performance; local policy determines documentation ownership and formal remediation tracking.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Defines refractory status after emergent and urgent therapy and provides adult midazolam loading, repeat-bolus, infusion, intubation, and continuous-EEG recommendations.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society Guidelines for the Evaluation and Management of Status Epilepticus
evidence.support: Supports continuous-infusion therapy titrated to electrographic seizure cessation and continued EEG monitoring in refractory status epilepticus.

seizure.risk: high: refractory status escalation and anesthetic dosing

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Emphasizes parallel emergency treatment, etiologic workup, and EEG assessment without delaying seizure control.

evidence.title: FDA Levetiracetam in Sodium Chloride Injection Prescribing Information (2024)
evidence.support: Provides adult renal-maintenance adjustment and the post-hemodialysis supplemental-dose requirement.

seizure.risk: high: separation of emergency loading from dialysis-adjusted maintenance

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: States that EEG is needed to identify nonconvulsive status in patients who do not recover and that continuous EEG is essential during refractory-status treatment.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society Status Epilepticus Guideline
evidence.support: Recommends continuous EEG to diagnose and monitor electrographic seizures, especially when clinical signs are absent or obscured.

seizure.risk: high: occult ongoing seizure after neuromuscular blockade

evidence.title: American Epilepsy Society Guideline for Treatment of Prolonged Seizures
evidence.support: Recommends an adequately dosed benzodiazepine in the 5- to 20-minute phase and prompt transition to fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam when seizures persist.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Provides lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV up to 4 mg and levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV up to 4,500 mg, and warns that first-line benzodiazepines are frequently underdosed.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Lists adult urgent-control options and doses, including levetiracetam 60 mg/kg up to 4,500 mg, with agent choice based on patient factors.

evidence.title: ESETT Randomized Trial by Age Group
evidence.support: Supports levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate as potential second-line choices after adequate benzodiazepine therapy, without a universal efficacy winner.

evidence.title: FDA CEREBYX Prescribing Information (2022)
evidence.support: Contains the boxed cardiovascular warning for IV fosphenytoin and requires cardiac and blood-pressure monitoring during and after administration.

seizure.risk: high: individualized second-line selection in shock and liver disease

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Provides the current refractory-status sequence: intubation, continuous anesthetic, continuous EEG, continuation of second-line therapy, and parallel etiologic evaluation.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society Status Epilepticus Guideline
evidence.support: Recommends continuous-infusion antiseizure therapy titrated to EEG goals and maintenance of electrographic control before slow withdrawal.

evidence.title: American Epilepsy Society Convulsive Status Epilepticus Guidance
evidence.support: Supports anesthetic-dose midazolam, propofol, pentobarbital, or thiopental with continuous EEG when second therapy fails.

seizure.risk: high: refractory status sequencing and etiologic parallel workup

evidence.title: FDA Levetiracetam in Sodium Chloride Injection Prescribing Information (2024)
evidence.support: Specifies adult ESRD maintenance dosing and a 250- to 500-mg supplemental dose after dialysis.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Supports prompt emergency treatment followed by ongoing antiseizure therapy and patient-specific dosing review.

seizure.risk: high: dialysis-related breakthrough seizure from omitted supplement

evidence.title: FDA DIPRIVAN Prescribing Information (2022)
evidence.support: Describes propofol infusion syndrome manifestations and risk factors, including prolonged doses above 5 mg/kg/hour for more than 48 hours and concomitant vasoconstrictors.

evidence.title: Neurocritical Care Society ENLS Status Epilepticus Protocol, Version 6.0 (2024)
evidence.support: Supports continuous anesthetic therapy with continuous EEG for refractory status and recognizes the need to individualize agent selection and monitoring.

seizure.risk: high: propofol infusion syndrome during refractory-status therapy
